heb.paragraph: Ishmael "struck down" (hikka) Gedaliah while eating together (v.2). The verb hikka with the phrase "by the sword" denotes murder, and the setting -- at a shared meal -- makes it treachery of the worst kind. Ancient Near Eastern hospitality law made violence against a dinner guest sacrilege. Ishmael also kills the Babylonian soldiers stationed at Mizpah and a group of pilgrims from the north.

refs.note: The parallel account of the assassination -- brief but confirming Jeremiah's detailed narrative.
"Even my close friend, someone I trusted, who shared my bread, has turned against me" -- the psalm of betrayal at table.

notes.note: Ishmael "of the royal family" -- his motive is likely political: he represents the old Davidic establishment that refuses to accept Babylonian rule. The king of Ammon backs him (40:14), adding a foreign dimension to the plot.

The murder of the pilgrims: eighty men from Shechem, Shiloh, and Samaria come with offerings for the ruined temple. Ishmael lures them in and kills seventy, sparing ten who offer hidden stores of food. The savagery is incomprehensible.
The bodies are thrown into a cistern "that King Asa had made" -- desecrating a structure built as defense against Baasha of Israel (1 Kgs 15:22). History layers upon history.

Calvin calls the assassination "the crowning tragedy of the book. When the one leader willing to follow God's counsel through Jeremiah is murdered, the last hope for peaceful existence dies with him."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The "seventh month" corresponds to Tishrei (September/October). The Jewish fast commemorating Gedaliah's murder (Tsom Gedaliah) falls on the third of Tishrei, the day after Rosh Hashanah.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies Ishmael's violence as "the death of the Jeremianic option." With Gedaliah gone, the policy of peaceful submission to Babylon -- the path Jeremiah advocated throughout -- becomes politically impossible.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the assassination as "violence consuming itself." Having destroyed the external enemy's patience and the internal leader's governance, Judah is left with neither structure nor hope.

hist.context: The assassination of Gedaliah in the seventh month (October 586 or 585 BC) is one of the most senseless acts in Israelite history. Ishmael, of royal blood, murders the governor during a meal, kills the Babylonian garrison (guaranteeing Babylonian reprisal), slaughters seventy of eighty pilgrims coming to mourn at the ruined temple site, and takes the remnant captive. The Fast of Gedaliah (Tsom Gedaliah) is still observed in Judaism on the third of Tishrei, testifying to the lasting trauma of this event.

heb.paragraph: The remnant camps at Geruth Kimham near Bethlehem (v.17), preparing to flee to Egypt. The place name gerut ("lodging") suggests temporary accommodation -- they are already in transit, no longer rooted in the land. The geography moves southward: Mizpah to Bethlehem to Egypt. Each step takes the people farther from the land God gave them.

refs.note: The king "must not send people back to Egypt" -- the Torah prohibition against returning to the house of bondage that the remnant is about to violate.
"Will they not return to Egypt? Will not Assyria rule over them?" -- Hosea's warning of reversed exodus.

notes.note: Johanan rescues the captives but cannot catch Ishmael. The assassin escapes to Ammon -- beyond the reach of Judean justice.

The flight to Egypt is motivated by fear: "They were afraid of them because Ishmael had killed Gedaliah, whom the king of Babylon had appointed." Babylonian reprisal -- real or imagined -- drives the decision.
Calvin identifies the root error: "Fear rather than faith determines their course. They consult their own panic rather than seeking God's word. This pattern has defined the entire national tragedy."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Geruth Kimham ("lodging of Kimham") may be connected to the Kimham of 2 Samuel 19:37-40, suggesting a traditional stopping place on the road south.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads the southward movement as "a geographical reversal of salvation history." Israel's story began with exodus FROM Egypt; it now threatens to end with flight TO Egypt. The narrative moves backward.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies the Egypt decision as "the final rejection of prophetic counsel." Every crisis in the book has been met with the same response: run from God's word rather than obey it.

hist.context: Johanan and the army officers pursue Ishmael and rescue the captives at Gibeon, but Ishmael escapes to Ammon with eight men. The rescued remnant, fearing Babylonian reprisal for Gedaliah's murder, decides to flee to Egypt -- the very destination Jeremiah has warned against throughout his ministry. The irony is cruel: escaping Babylon by running to the other empire that God has condemned.

themes.note: Judgment at 8 -- the assassination and its aftermath. Justice at 7: the violation of hospitality and murder of innocents.

lit.note: The assassination destroys the last viable political structure in Judah and triggers the flight to Egypt that Jeremiah will desperately oppose.

hebtext.note: Ishmael "struck down" (hikka) Gedaliah while eating together (v.2). The verb hikka with the phrase "by the sword" denotes murder, and the setting -- at a shared meal -- makes it treachery of the worst kind. Ancient Near Eastern hospitality law made violence against a dinner guest sacrilege. Ishmael also kills the Babylonian soldiers stationed at Mizpah and a group of pilgrims from the north.

The remnant camps at Geruth Kimham near Bethlehem (v.17), preparing to flee to Egypt. The place name gerut ("lodging") suggests temporary accommodation -- they are already in transit, no longer rooted in the land. The geography moves southward: Mizpah to Bethlehem to Egypt. Each step takes the people farther from the land God gave them.

ppl.text: The assassination of Gedaliah in the seventh month (October 586 or 585 BC) is one of the most senseless acts in Israelite history…

ppl.name: Ishmael
ppl.text: Ishmael, of royal blood, murders the governor during a meal, kills the Babylonian garrison (guaranteeing Babylonian reprisal), slaughters seventy of eighty pilgrims coming to mourn at the ruined temple site, and takes the remnant captive…

translations.text: But ten of them said to Ishmael, “Don’t kill us! We have wheat and barley, olive oil and honey, hidden in a field.” So he let them alone and did not kill them with the others.

But there were ten men among them who said to Ishmael, “Do not put us to death, for we have stores of wheat, barley, oil, and honey hidden in the fields.” So he refrained and did not put them to death with their companions.

Now the cistern where he threw all the bodies of the men he had killed along with Gedaliah was the one King Asa had made as part of his defense against Baasha king of Israel. Ishmael son of Nethaniah filled it with the dead.

Now the cistern into which Ishmael had thrown all the bodies of the men whom he had struck down along with Gedaliah was the large cistern that King Asa had made for defense against Baasha king of Israel; Ishmael the son of Nethaniah filled it with the slain.

rec.quote: "The "seventh month" corresponds to Tishrei (September/October). The Jewish fast commemorating Gedaliah's murder (Tsom Gedaliah) falls on the third of Tishrei, the day after Rosh Hashanah."

thread.text: The parallel account of the assassination -- brief but confirming Jeremiah's detailed narrative.
"Even my close friend, someone I trusted, who shared my bread, has turned against me" -- the psalm of betrayal at table.
The king "must not send people back to Egypt" -- the Torah prohibition against returning to the house of bondage that the remnant is about to violate.
"Will they not return to Egypt? Will not Assyria rule over them?" -- Hosea's warning of reversed exodus.

coaching.tip: Ishmael murders Gedaliah at the table — killing his host during a meal (v. 1). Table fellowship was sacred in the ancient Near East; violating it was the ultimate betrayal. The detail echoes Judas's betrayal at the Last Supper. Every hope of rebuilding dies with Gedaliah. Jeremiah's narrative shows how violence, not Babylon, destroys the remnant from within.

The remnant now fears Babylonian retaliation for Gedaliah's murder and prepares to flee to Egypt — the very move Jeremiah has consistently warned against. The pattern is relentless: each crisis produces the same response (flight to Egypt) and each time God says don't go. Jeremiah 41 sets up the final disobedience in chapters 42–43. The story's circularity is its judgment: Israel keeps choosing what has always failed.
